text:
Hi @DrBitcoinM50862 , good to hear from you and glad you support the decision.

Good question.

The quarterly yield is a snapshot of BPS (Bitcoin per share) at that point in time and its not intended to be a permanent number.

Debt-funded purchases will increase BPS initially, and if/when that debt is repaid (via equity or cash flow), that will naturally flow back through the fully diluted share count and be reflected in BPS in that period.

So the uplift is real based on today’s share count, but it’s not the end state. The outcome over time depends on how the debt is managed, the price of any (potential) equity issued and how our Bitcoin holdings grow. 

Hope that helps and all is well with you.

engagement.note: These figures were correct as of May 14, 2026, 7:08 AM London time

We’ve acquired 28₿, taking our total to 2,778₿.

These are always my favourite updates. Nothing beats adding more Bitcoin to the war chest, increasing Bitcoin exposure per share (QTD yield: 12.98%).

Total drawn: £13.5m,  Leverage ratio: ~9.3%.

Our facility remains competitive and flexible: 6.75–7.25% variable rate and repayable at our discretion, no additional charges

As a reminder, we  recently updated our Bitcoin treasury policy (24/4/26) to allow us to use the facility to acquire Bitcoin and introduce measured, responsible leverage following investor feedback. 

The next stage of evolution is clear for Bitcoin Treasury companies - the market wants to see amplification/leverage (vs relying purely on sentiment) and we believe this model supports a more sustained premium to mNAV over time. As always, we will manage this responsibly and inline with market conditions.

text:
Morning all - we’ve released an RNS relating to our LTIP (Long Term Incentive Plan) and want to share more context.  

We have a 10-year plan to build one of the leading companies in the UK. That requires patience and commitment, which is why it’s so important we have a team that’s fully aligned, especially as we enter year 2.  

That’s the thinking behind our LTIP, announced following strong shareholder support at the AGM.  

In practice, that means:  

• Awards linked to ambitious market cap and share price milestones (not to be used as forecasts). 
• Awards that vest over multiple years, in tranches (minimum 5-year period). 
• No awards that vest or become exercisable for at least the first 3 years. 
• Full transparency - all participants and award levels have been disclosed.  

This keeps us focused on the bigger picture, with every decision coming back to one question, "what's in the best long term interest of our shareholders?"   

If we succeed, we succeed together.

LSE: #SWC | OTCQB: $TSWCF | FRA: $3M8

text:
Last year The Smarter Web Company  earned a reputation as "the fastest horse" - building momentum quickly and establishing our position in the Bitcoin treasury sector as the UK leader.

As we move into year 2 of our 10-year plan, the focus evolves - increasingly, the priority is a capital structure that is simple, easy to explain and straightforward for investors to understand.

Our aim is to build the cleanest, most transparent capital structure in the sector.

Across the industry, growth has often been supported by complex instruments such as warrants, convertibles or structured deals. 

We used some of these in 2025 where it felt appropriate, and they played a role in getting us to where we are today. We wouldn't rule anything out entirely, but any future use would need to clear a high bar - delivering a clear and meaningful benefit to shareholders.

The Bitcoin treasury model is still widely misunderstood. Concepts like "accretive dilution" and "Bitcoin per share" are often viewed through a traditional equity and credit lens, which makes the next evolution of the model, built around "amplification" via preferred equity, even harder to communicate.

Against that backdrop, a clean capital structure, a healthy free float and strong liquidity are key to making our equity story easier to understand and more attractive to long-term institutional capital. It may mean accumulating Bitcoin more gradually at times, but it helps build the resilience needed to go the distance.

Building this vision takes time and patience, but I believe we have a clear, realistic plan to execute it. Removing a large portion of our pre-IPO warrants and the perceived overhang was a first step in that process.

There is sophistication in simplicity and that is the equity story we are building at Smarter Web.

LSE: #SWC | OTCQB: $TSWCF | FRA: $3M8

text:
GM All. A Bitcoin purchase RNS feels like a fitting way to mark our one-year anniversary.

This morning we've announced the purchase of 44 BTC, taking our total to 2,750 and QTD Bitcoin yield to 11.84%.

Alongside this, we’ve updated our Bitcoin Treasury Policy to allow the use of our strategic credit facility with Coinbase to fund Bitcoin purchases over time. The facility allows us to continue increasing our leverage in a responsible manner, against a backdrop of favourable conditions for taking on additional debt supported by a competitive loan structure. The purchase was funded in part through that facility. 

With Bitcoin still ~40% below its October highs and our leverage previously at ~6.4%, we believe selective use of leverage at this stage of the cycle is an effective way to accelerate accumulation and support a sustained mNAV premium.

More broadly, we see measured leverage (or “amplification”), when used responsibly, as an important component of a modern Bitcoin treasury strategy - one that we expect to become increasingly common across the industry, with levels actively managed through the cycle.

Following this purchase, total drawings under the facility are £12.0m, with leverage at ~8.1%. The facility is secured against our Bitcoin holdings and has no fixed maturity, giving us full flexibility over repayment timing. For full transparency, we have decided to disclose the current variable interest rate we are paying, which is between 6.75% and 7.25%.

We believe this reflects a disciplined, responsible approach and is part of our ongoing capital structure optimisation strategy.

LSE: #SWC | OTCQB: $TSWCF | FRA: $3M8

text:
GM all.

An administrative RNS this morning. 

We’ve put in place a mechanism with the LSE that allows us to issue up to ~54 million new shares in the future if all pre-IPO warrants are exercised (remember ~27m are owned by Andrew, his wife and directors/employees of SWC). 

This is standard practice. No shares are being issued now - this is just an administrative step to make future issuance straightforward if and when warrants are exercised (window opens from 24 April 2026 to 28 April 2028). 

We will be updating the market at intervals (a company has up to 60 days) to make sure we’re being as transparent as possible on how many warrants have been exercised.

Hope that makes sense.

Thanks and let’s have a great day.

text:
Call for action: Need ground troops to mobilize.

Need your help Smarters.

SWC LN was the #5 top riser in the FTSE All-Share today (+6.1%).

However, this is still not being correctly reflected on certain platforms (e.g. @ii_couk, @Investcentre / AJ Bell, Fidelity), which source data from @MorningstarInc .

The key issues are:

1. SWC LN is not consistently listed as a constituent of the FTSE All-Share and/or FTSE Small Cap.
2. Price performance is not being captured in top risers/fallers lists.

We’ve been trying to resolve this for several days but it's still not resolved - any support in escalating this would be greatly appreciated.

Please can you email the below email to support.uk@morningstar.com so they hear us.
----------------------------------------------------------

Subject: Urgent: SWC LN Data Representation – FTSE Constituency & Performance

Hi Morningstar Team,

I’m writing regarding the data representation of The Smarter Web Company (SWC LN, listed on the LSE), which still does not appear to be correctly reflected across a number of platforms that rely on Morningstar data.

For example, SWC LN was the #5 top riser in the FTSE All-Share on 22 April (+6.1%), however this is not being captured on platforms such as Interactive Investor, AJ Bell and Fidelity.

The key issues appear to be:

1. SWC LN not consistently listed as a constituent of the FTSE All-Share and FTSE Small Cap indices
2. Performance not being reflected in “top risers/fallers” lists

For clarity, SWC LN is a constituent of both indices following our admission earlier this year.

It is important for both our shareholders and broader market transparency that this information is accurately reflected. We would greatly appreciate your assistance in investigating and resolving this as soon as possible.

Please let me know if any further information is required from our side.

Kind regards,

text:
Every great business solves a problem.  

In todays economic backdrop, saving (let alone investing) is getting harder as households everywhere are feeling the pinch. 

Many people are asking two similar questions  

1. “How can I earn more money to invest (in Bitcoin)?” 
 
2. “How do I increase my exposure to the best performing asset of the last decade (Bitcoin)?”  

This is where a well-run Bitcoin treasury company comes in. Our value proposition is to solve those two problems by increasing how much Bitcoin exposure our shares represent over time.   

As a listed company on the LSE, @smarterwebuk has access to equity, debt, and other capital sources. That allows us to acquire more Bitcoin so each share you own represents a growing amount of Bitcoin over the long term.  

A simple example:  

If Bitcoin goes from $100k → $200k, your fiat value doubles but your Bitcoin exposure stays the same.

Now compare that to a treasury model:  If Bitcoin per share grows at ~20% per year, over 10 years your Bitcoin exposure grows by 5.2x.  

So it’s not just about Bitcoin going up,  it’s about ending up with more over time without having to keep putting in new cash, freeing up your money for other things that matter.

Our role is not only to amplify Bitcoin's price performance but to grow underlying exposure over time for our shareholders.

LSE: #SWC | OTCQB: $TSWCF | FRA: $3M8

text:
Hi Derin, thanks for your comment.

To clarify, the quarterly yield is not being positioned as a permanent number, it’s simply a snapshot of our Bitcoin per share / Quarterly
Yield immediately after the transaction.

Yes, the debt does need to be repaid, and that come will come via equity issuance (and/or operating cash flow as per our RNS) which will flow into the FD total share count number. 

Where this differs from the warrant overhang is that the warrants represented uncertain, market driven dilution where we would have no control, whereas the debt (+interest) is something we can actively manage in terms of timing and how it’s refinanced. That gives us more control over the outcome, even though the cost itself doesn’t disappear.

Using your example, if nothing else changes, yes, the yield would naturally come down once the debt is repaid. In practice however the end result is driven by two things from here:

- the price at which equity is issued (which determines the new number of FD shares) 
- how our Bitcoin stack grows over that period which the example didn’t account for. 

So the increase isn’t an “illusion”, it’s a real improvement based on today’s share count but it’s also not the final state. The end result will reflect how/when the debt is paid alongside any growth in our Bitcoin holdings.

From a reporting perspective, we let Bitcoin per share / yield move each period on a fully diluted basis.  As the debt is repaid and the share count evolves, that comes through in the numbers alongside any change in Bitcoin holdings.

Hope that helps.

engagement.note: These figures were correct as of May 14, 2026, 7:08 AM London time

text:
It’s a good question.

Debt isn’t directly included in the BPS calculation, that’s simply Bitcoin held divided by shares outstanding at a point in time.

Where it does come in is through what happens when the debt is repaid.

In practice, repaying the debt will usually involve issuing equity (via shares) or cash that could otherwise have gone into Bitcoin (eg from our operating business). So the impact of the debt shows up in one of two ways:

1. More shares outstanding which reduces BPS.

2. Less Bitcoin accumulated which also reduces BPS

At the same time, if we’re adding Bitcoin to the balance sheet, that pushes BPS higher. So there are really two moving parts: share count and Bitcoin held.

That’s why it’s best to think of BPS as a snapshot in time rather than a fixed number - it reflects where you are based on those two variables.

While debt isn’t explicitly in the formula, it is fully reflected over time as those variables evolve.

The way we approach it is to let BPS move each period on a fully diluted basis. As debt is repaid and the share count changes, that comes through in the quarterly numbers alongside any growth in the Bitcoin holdings.

I hope that helps explain it. It’s been good chatting and look forward to speaking more soon.

text:
How we calculate Bitcoin yield:

Total bitcoin holdings = 2,695 = 269,500,000,000 satoshis

Satoshis before transaction:
680 sats/share

New Fully diluted share count after buying back 39m warrants  = 357,040,063 (396,040,063 -39,000,000)

Satoshis per share = 269,500,000,000 ÷ 357,040,063  = 755 

Bitcoin yield:
755 ÷ 680 = 1.109 = +10.9%

text:
Note from @Tennyson_Secs 

Buy-Back of Pre-IPO Warrants

The UK’s flagship Bitcoin Treasury Company, The Smarter Web Company has announced the buy-back of 39 million pre-IPO warrants following yesterday’s announcement of a discounted voluntary purchase offer.   

The total consideration of £8,775,000 was based on an offer price of 22,5 pence per warrant, a significant discount to yesterday’s closing price for the shares, and the effective price paid represented an mNAV of just 0.7x, making it an attractive outcome for the company.   

$SWC financed the deal through drawing funds from the $30 million Coinbase Strategic Credit Facility it announced in February, and said it intended to repay this short-term debt through “a mixture of operational cash flow and future equity. issuance”.  

In terms of metrics, the company’s total debt of £9.5 million now represents 6.6% of its overall NAV.  But with the number of fully diluted shares reducing by 9.85%, and the buyback price being at such a discount, the company has now delivered a QTD Bitcoin yield of 10.92%, putting it at the top of the leaderboard globally.

As important as the boost to Bitcoin yield from the transaction, the deal also removes the perception of a short-term overhang depressing the share price.  

Of the 95 million original pre-IPO warrants, there are now just 54,066,335 2.5p warrants remining, of which 25,778,732 are held by Andrew Webley & Family, and a further 1,450,000 are held by directors or employees of the company.   

With the exercise date approaching later this month, it is highly likely that the other warrant holders who chose not to accept the offer this week will remain investors in the company post the exercise date.

Since its IPO in April 2025, SWC has increased its BTC per share by almost 700x.

text:
Smarter Web was one of the most volatile stocks across the FTSE All-Share during its first 30 trading days on the LSE (prior to its inclusion in the index on 23 March 2026).

Over our first 30 trading days (4 Feb – 17 Mar), our equity had an approximate ~114% annualised realized volatility. This equates to an approximate 7.2% average daily move.

According to Bloomberg, this ranked as the 6th most volatile stock in the FTSE All-Share over that period.

We view volatility as a sign of vitality. 

The UK market has historically been characterised by defensive, lower-volatility stocks, and we believe there is a role for a higher-growth, higher-volatility profile within active portfolios.

Thanks, Andrew - it’s an incredibly challenging environment for asset managers and capital allocators right now. 

A lot of participants are sitting on elevated cash levels, and in conversations with traders and PMs, it’s clear gross exposure has been materially reduced, which aligns with the price action we’ve seen across equities.

That said, I’m genuinely proud of what the team and broader community accomplished in Q1. I feel confident in our plan for Q2 and am looking forward to executing it alongside you and @Croesus_BTC. 

As you mentioned, there’s a bigger mission underway, and everyone has a role to play  let’s keep pushing forward together.

text:
I always like the start of a new quarter. Q1 was clearly challenging for markets and sentiment, but there’s a lot of company-specific, tangible progress to carry into Q2 and remain constructive about.

As @asjwebley mentioned in his weekly update, if your long-term view on Bitcoin remains bullish, there will be a place for Bitcoin treasury companies but we have to realistic that the timing of when sentiment shifts is very hard to predict given the current backdrop. My view remains that the global debasement trade continues for many reasons, and Bitcoin remains the best expression of that.

Thank you to everyone in our community and our shareholders for the ongoing support. We are on a mission to raise awareness that Bitcoin (and Bitcoin on a corporate balance sheet) is a positive for both private and public companies. It will take time, but we, our community and UK peers believe strongly in this long-term shift.

Onwards into Q2. Heads down, keep building.
